output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.6, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: Rewrote to scope the claims to what the experiments actually support, cut the unsupported generalizations, and flagged issues that need author input before this can be tightened further.

## Main problems in the current text

1. **The headline claim is much broader than the evaluation.** The abstract says CandidatePicker "produces type-correct programs across the full breadth of statically-typed functional programming." The benchmark is first-order, over a five-type fragment `{Int, Bool, String, List<Int>, List<String>}`, with no ADTs, polymorphism, type classes, modules, or higher-order functions. This is not the breadth of statically-typed functional programming; it is a small, monomorphic fragment. The same overreach reappears in the conclusion ("transfer directly to production functional codebases ... Haskell and OCaml") and in the "principal contribution" sentence. These need to be scoped.

2. **"Eliminating the previously reported tradeoff between candidate diversity and aggregation fidelity"** is asserted with no citation and no ablation that isolates diversity from fidelity. Either cite the tradeoff (whose result? what setting?) and show the relevant measurement, or drop the claim.

3. **The reward signal includes a hidden-test pass rate, and the evaluation reports a functional-correctness rate.** If the reward's "hidden tests" overlap with the evaluation tests — or are drawn from the same reference set per task — this is leakage and the 81.3% number is not interpretable as held-out functional correctness. The paper must state that these test sets are disjoint, or report results with a reward trained only on type-check signal.

4. **"Matching or exceeding monolithic synthesis baselines on every task category we evaluate"** — the body only reports aggregate numbers (94.7% / 81.3%) plus a qualitative remark about list-manipulation tasks. The per-category claim needs a table or it should be removed.

5. **The per-agent ablation arithmetic doesn't close.** "Each of the five contributes the winning candidate on at least 14% of tasks" lower-bounds the total at 70%. What accounts for the remaining 30% — ties broken arbitrarily, no-winner cases, or a sixth source? State this.

6. **HumanEval and MBPP are Python benchmarks.** How were tasks ported to a typed functional fragment? Were signatures hand-translated, auto-translated, filtered? Without this, the 1,500-task count is unauditable.

7. **No citations.** "Prior synthesis-based aggregation," "the standard multi-agent pipeline," and the diversity/fidelity tradeoff all need references.

## Suggested revision

> We introduce CandidatePicker, a selection-based aggregator for multi-agent LLM program synthesis. In contrast to synthesis-based aggregators [CITE], which blend multiple proposals into a single output, CandidatePicker retains the highest-scoring individual candidate. On a benchmark of typed first-order functional tasks over the type fragment `{Int, Bool, String, List<Int>, List<String>}`, CandidatePicker matches or exceeds a monolithic synthesis baseline and the strongest individual agent.
>
> **Setup.** Five base agents (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, Llama-3.1-405B, DeepSeek-V2, Mixtral-8x22B) each produce one candidate per task. CandidatePicker ranks candidates with a learned reward combining type-checker pass rate and pass rate on a held-out subset of unit tests *disjoint from the evaluation tests* [CONFIRM]. The benchmark comprises 1,500 tasks: [N₁] hand-translated from HumanEval, [N₂] from MBPP, and [N₃] hand-authored in OCaml. Each task specifies an input/output signature and a reference test set. Tasks are first-order; argument and return types are drawn from the fixed fragment above. Recursion is permitted, and helper functions may be introduced provided they inhabit the same fragment.
>
> **Results.** Across all 1,500 tasks, CandidatePicker reaches 94.7% type correctness and 81.3% functional correctness, versus 88.1% / 71.0% for the best single agent and 83.4% / 64.2% for the synthesis aggregator. Gains are largest on list-manipulation tasks (Table X), where selection preserves a single coherent recursion pattern rather than averaging across incompatible ones. Per-agent ablations show that no single agent dominates: each of the five wins on at least 14% of tasks, with ties resolved by [METHOD] on the remaining [X%].
>
> **Scope.** Our evaluation is restricted to first-order programs over a small monomorphic type fragment. We do not evaluate on richer features used in production Haskell and OCaml — polymorphism, ADTs, type classes, modules, or higher-order functions — and we do not claim the result transfers to those settings without further study.
>
> **Contribution.** We show that, within this fragment, selection-based aggregation outperforms synthesis-based aggregation on both type correctness and functional correctness, and that the gain is not attributable to any one base agent. We release CandidatePicker, the benchmark, and the trained reward model.

## Items I could not resolve from the text alone

- The exact composition of the 1,500 tasks across HumanEval / MBPP / OCaml.
- Whether the reward's hidden tests are disjoint from the evaluation tests.
- The per-category numbers needed to support the "every task category" claim.
- Which prior works are meant by "synthesis-based aggregation" and the diversity/fidelity tradeoff.

These should be filled in by the authors rather than guessed; my revision marks each with `[CITE]`, `[CONFIRM]`, or a bracketed placeholder.

Uncertainty: I have not verified that the cited prior-work category ("synthesis-based aggregation") has a canonical reference in this literature, nor that HumanEval/MBPP have standard typed-functional ports — both should be checked before submission.
